# Trial Task

I chose the banking domain because it's directly relevant to my current work at Al Rajhi Bank. The test cases, metrics, and edge cases are all grounded to my experience.

**Q1: Prompt Reliability**

* The core idea was to test around a concrete fact rather than open-ended generation: a numeric policy answer is either right or wrong, which makes scoring clean and unambiguous. I picked one real Al Rajhi policy: the minimum deposit is SAR 1 within 90 days.  
* I researched common RAG failure modes and built 10 cases around them. The first five are synthetic, generated by applying four transformations to the base question — synonym substitution, sentence inversion, filler noise, and false premise injection. The last five come from real production experience — Arabic input, multi-intent queries, hallucination on out-of-corpus questions, heavy typos, and temporal reasoning.  
* Cases T1–T4 form the invariance group. They all ask the same thing differently and are scored against the same reference string — if noise or rephrasing causes the model to say SAR 500 instead of SAR 1, semantic similarity catches it. T5 is the perturbation test — a user injects a false claim and the model must correct it, not confirm it.  
* For scoring I used semantic similarity across all 10 cases with a single consistent metric at threshold 0.6. I chose one scorer deliberately — mixing regex and semantic across cases makes the harness harder to reason about and harder to explain.  
* For production I'd ship the invariance group and CLI report first as they give the highest signal fastest. Later I'd add an LLM-as-judge layer for nuanced failures, auto-generated paraphrases to expand the test set, and CI integration to catch regressions on every prompt change. I'd also layer DeepEval, RAGAS on top for a more structured test suite if time allowed.

**Q2: Retrieval \+ Guardrails \+ FastAPI**

* For the **data**, instead of writing clean snippets I simulated how RAG works in the real world. You get a messy document, you chunk it, you index it. I wrote one raw policy document and built a sentence-based chunker with overlap so facts don't get cut across chunk boundaries. Each chunk also carries metadata so you can trace any retrieved snippet back to its exact location in the source document.  
* For the architecture I separated concerns into three layers: **rag pipeline** for indexing and retrieval, **routes** for the API and guardrails, and **scripts** for offline utilities like chunking and config comparison. The index is built once at startup via FastAPI lifespan and stored in memory as a numpy array. Everything is async throughout, Async Groq client, async route, so the server never blocks on I/O.  
* For **guardrails** I chose three that make sense specifically for banking. A denylist blocks known sensitive terms before retrieval even runs. PII detection uses regex to catch national IDs, phone numbers, and IBANs. You never want personal data logged in plain text in a banking system. A confidence threshold fires after retrieval. If the top score is below 0.4 we refuse to answer rather than hallucinate. I chose the confidence threshold as the primary guardrail because it directly addresses the most common RAG failure: answering confidently when the corpus has nothing relevant.  
* For the **index** comparison I tested cosine vs dot product across four queries at k=3. Both retrieved the same top chunk every time, confirming semantic agreement. Cosine scores are bounded between 0 and 1 making them directly usable as a confidence threshold, while dot product scores are unbounded and vary with query length, so cosine is the cleaner production choice. I considered comparing k=3 vs k=5 as well but with only 21 chunks the difference doesn't surface meaningfully. That comparison becomes relevant at larger corpus sizes where answers genuinely span multiple chunks.  
* **Monitoring** is sketched via two structured log lines on every request, latency and top retrieval score. In production these would ship to CloudWatch with alerts on p95 latency and retrieval hit rate. If the hit rate drops below 80% it signals the corpus needs expanding, with no additional dependencies needed at the prototype stage.  
* The **main trade-off** I made was spending most of my time on the **architecture** and **skeleton** rather than optimizing retrieval quality. I wanted the codebase to be production-minded from the start: clean separation of concerns, async throughout, guardrails as their own layer, config driven by environment variables. The foundation is solid and the optimizations are straightforward to layer on top.
